title: Async/Await Fundamentals
content:
Rust's async/await system lets you write concurrent code that looks sequential. An `async fn` returns a `Future` -- a value representing a computation that will complete later. Futures are lazy: they do nothing until you `.await` them.

Unlike Go's goroutines or JavaScript's promises that start immediately, Rust futures are inert values. This is a deliberate design choice: it gives you full control over when and where work happens.

The `.await` keyword suspends the current async function until the future completes, yielding control back to the executor so other tasks can run. This is cooperative multitasking -- your async code must periodically await to let other tasks proceed.

Key mental model: async/await is syntactic sugar for state machines. The compiler transforms your async function into a state machine that implements the Future trait.

codeExamples.description: Futures are lazy -- nothing happens without .await

keyPoints: async fn returns a Future, not the value directly
Futures are lazy -- they do nothing until polled (awaited)
.await suspends the current task and yields to the executor
The compiler transforms async functions into state machines
You need a runtime (like tokio) to actually execute futures
